summary: Jeff Atwood argues that desktop backgrounds are fundamentally superfluous because a computer in active use should have its screen covered with productive applications, not decorative wallpaper. He views visible desktop pixels as wasted real estate and treats seeing his background as a 'reprimand' for poor monitor utilization. However, he acknowledges the fun of having a unique background and permits occasional indulgence, provided users remember the desktop is merely an 'airport' to pass through, not a destination. The post concludes with extensive wallpaper resources, delivered with self-aware irony given his stated philosophy.
key_points: If you're actively using your computer, your desktop should be completely obscured by productive applications and data
Visible desktop background represents wasted screen real estate and poor utilization of monitor pixels
Obsessing over desktop aesthetics risks treating computers as fashion accessories rather than tools
The desktop should be viewed as an 'aesthetically pleasing airport' - a brief waypoint, not a destination
Multi-monitor setups require specialized utilities like Ultramon or Display Fusion to properly manage backgrounds across screens
money_quotes: If you're really using your computer, your desktop should almost never be visible.
Every time the desktop background is visible, that means I'm making poor use of my monitor pixels.
Whenever the desktop background peeks through, I treat it like a reprimand.
I worry that if we spend too much time obsessing over our desktop backgrounds, we'll start treating our computers like fashion accessories instead of tools.
We should be filling our screens with information, not distracting ourselves with pretty frippery.
The desktop is like an aesthetically pleasing airport we must occasionally pass through before arriving at our real destinations.
Just don't go so far that you become one of those weird people who hangs out in airports.
key_insight: Your computer's desktop background is a waypoint, not a workspace - any time you can see it, you're not actually working.
